speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see Reina right away. We went through the whole procedure of checking in, as they call it-- which is pretty awful, and dumb, at the same time. I think the worst was that, it not only took forever-- for no reason whatsoever-- but you had, then, also-- some of the stuff was that you had to undress completely. And the ones who checked you for more than a hidden ring-- but for diamonds-- in like little cubicles, when you go to doctor. Men and women were not separated, which didn't matter anyway. You had only one hanger to hang your clothes. It was very fast. And you stood facing the wall in a knee-bent position, legs spread apart. And these animals, those two horse-teeth looking Belgian guards, checked. And whoever protested got the worst of it. So this is the first thing you learn-- you don't protest. You do it as fast as possible. Get in and out.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had an old woman, and they truly, truly manhandled her to such a degree, it was pathetic. Her husband wanted to get them away from her. They dragged her husband-- who held onto her leg and said, don't go with them, don't go with them. And all the while I kept thinking, what about the nine-months-old baby? What are they going to do with the baby? Or with that elderly couple? Are they going make her undress-- a lady, truly a lady? And have her stand there, looking whether she had some hidden diamonds? Awful. You went through about five or six or seven. And you told them your name, and you told them your address. And it was all stupid. It was all stupid-- it didn't make sense because the girl behind the typewriter didn't paying any attention to it, didn't listen to it, no matter what you said. You could have said, I just came from the Mars, and I landed here, OK? It would have been OK, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you knew it didn't make any sense. But it was because so they had a better job. They could do that. And that took forever. And then you were outside. And I know that, when we first lined up, there was a man who said, the commander is coming. And first, you had two of the German Shepherds racing through, which scared the daylights out of you. And then came the commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went along the line and said, Jew, Jew, Jew, Jew, Jew. And when he came to me, I said no, I'm Mischling. And after he passed, I remember I had an envelope to give him. And it scared me, but I thought, if I don't give it to him, who knows what's going to happen? So at the end of the row, I said, Herr Kommandant, excuse me, I need to give you something. And my heart was going like this. And he took the envelope from me-- he never opened it. He handed it to the next guy and went on. And that was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the moment I said no, I'm not Jew, I'm-- I'm Mischling, they hung a sign around me with a big E. E4-- E means in German, what it meant is entscheidung-- decision. The decision has not been made yet. And the others got a number-- number such and such.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned really quickly that E was much better than a number, because number meant eventually transport. And E-- they have to decide.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody told me that they had people in there who had their E number for 10 months or so already. They still didn't have the papers. I had said my papers are in Hungary, and it takes a long time to come with the mail. You don't know where it's held up, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. I knew whatever they write it down, they don't write it down, that's what I said. So I was led into a barrack where the E people were, which was much better than the others. Not as far the amount of people who were there-- certainly not. But it was supposed to be-- you were safer. For the next two weeks, I can only tell you I paid no attention whatsoever to what was going on in the camp. I was too busy crying, and too busy having cramps. I got my period 10 days before I should have. And then did not have it anymore for two years. And I cried for two weeks, constantly, day and night. And after two weeks, I stopped crying and didn't cry anymore for two years either.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, when I first came, I thought I have landed in hell, and I'm not going to make it. Not another day. That was then. Because there was a wall around me. And I have always had-- I'd always the feeling the walls, every day, they come closer and closer. And before long, we were all going to be totally swallowed up by them. And that was absolutely-- I was sure that I could hear it, I could see it, I could feel the walls coming closer, when I was in the courtyard. And it was madness, absolute. I couldn't be fenced in. There was only one gate-- the gate you came in through, a big, huge iron gate, where two of those horrendous tall Belgian guards stood. Otherwise, it was like a military kasein. There was some--so many entrances, and each entrance had, on their second floor, left and right, for about I would say 500, 600 people, sleeping accommodation downstairs in the first floor. But it was a house. It was really a kasein, a house. It was not barracks. I'm sure that once upon a time, they were used by military. And all you had was a courtyard-- stones-- around that. You couldn't see over the houses. I have no idea what was on the outside. Whether we were smack in the middle of the city, or whether we were on the outskirts, I couldn't tell you. I mean, I couldn't have you told you then. There was no dining room, there was no recreation room. There was nothing. There were only these-- what you'd call-- pigeon holes, our sleeping quarters. I mean, like this. No room for storing anything. You had to, whatever, do as best as you can. Toilets was a catastrophe. There were two, outside on the hall. Men and women were together, couples were together, families were together, lovers or not lovers were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as that's concerned, as far as morale is concerned, you just hoped that everything would work out all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a [NON-ENGLISH]-- in other words, one was in charge of each of the barracks. It what his job to see that there were no fights, no arguments, no thievery going on, no lovemaking against somebody's will, or whatever. Food-- well, you know, if it is your first encounter with being imprisoned, everything is horrible. Looking back on it a few months later, it was heaven, because there were no guards' chambers there. We had one guy there, a Unterscharführer Boden, B-O-D-E-N. Whatever that means-- Unterscharführer. And he was not only a pig, but he was a sadist. And I think this is what he built his career on. And this man, contrary to norm, hated pretty girls. He didn't hate good-looking men, he didn't hate ugly girls, he hated good-looking girls. There was a passion in this hatred.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these girls he pursued and stalked. It was absolutely incredible. And I was warned about it from the beginning. So what are you going to do? Where am I going to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other thing was that you were allowed to keep your own clothes. There were no prison uniforms, or anything like that. And you could keep, you know, soap, and a washrag, or comb, and make-up, even. I mean, they took it away, but then somebody stole it and gave it back to you. This went on all along. They take it, you steal it, then you get it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: How was life? This guy was after me from day one. And there was one occasion when I had-- couldn't find my-- you know, it seems ridiculous to think of powder and lipstick in a situation like this. But it is precisely these little personal items which become terribly important. It's the only thing referenced to yourself, to personality, to who you are. And not to, you know, give into to just becoming one of the masses, looking like everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't find my powder compact. And they had in the morning stupid, stupid, exercises-- shoulder up, shoulder down, shoulder up, shoulder-- I mean stupid. Didn't do anybody any good. And I was looking for it. And when I was supposed to be down, I wasn't. And then I heard him call, Mr. Boden, Mr. Unterscharführer Boden, screaming, screaming, screaming. And I was afraid of the guy. I didn't know whether I should hide under whatever, or go down and face him. And then I was afraid he would come up. So I went down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, in front of everybody, started humiliating me. Madam, sorry to disturb you. You know, very, very, very ridiculous. And is there anything we can do for you? You're late this morning. Why are you late? Screaming. And I whispered-- I mean, how do you say now to the SS man, I lost my powder compact? I mean, it seems ridiculous, really. So I whispered. And he kept screaming. And everybody, about 700, 800 people, were, of course, staring. And then finally said, I can't find my powder compact. And he thought I was-- he didn't know what to make of it. You know, is she serious? Is she serious? A powder compact?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he whispered to somebody else. And this guy disappeared and came back a little while later. There's a whole big sack of flour. And that is thrown over my face, and over my head, and over me. And I was eating that bloody thing. And I was tasting it-- it was in my eyes. And another. And he thought he would help me out with my toilet, so that I have enough powder to last me for a long time. And I know that my eyes were burning, terribly burning. But I felt the humiliation much more than the pain-- you know, the physical pain. I stood there. And I figured this is, you know, absurd, really absurd. And then somebody took me, I know, and put me in a shower and lent me her clothes. And I figured, I don't know where to hide from him. I really don't know where to hide from him. And it was, again, something like you say, you are a victim there. You don't have to do anything, you don't have to do anything. And if he so choose it, he can do what he wants with you. We were not physically, otherwise except for this creep, attacked. But you know, the closeness-- you suddenly sleep next to strangers. You breathe down at strangers. When you turn, they have to turn. There is no privacy anymore-- none. Of course, I heard then the stories of the others who had come the same route I had come-- many, many, many. This Harry and John had brought about-- and Max-- about 150 over. I was the last one, I'm happy to say. The same way I came. The whole way with Reina in the same barrack. And then there was an occasion-- oh, yes. There-- there was a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was in-- going in between the commander and us, to see that they don't do too bad things to us, whatever. He was an operatic tenor, who didn't, I hear-- he didn't have to be there. He volunteered. He was married to a non-Jewish women, lived in Brussels, and volunteered to see that things went all right. A very nice man. The go-between us, let's say, and then the kommandant. And he had told-- and there was one day when, suddenly, when I was walking in the courtyard, I heard a lot of screaming and yelling and pointing at the gate. And the gate was open. And outside stood Margit-- my non-Jewish friend from Amsterdam, waving papers. On my life, as true as I'm sitting here-- Silvia, Silvia, I've got your papers, I've got your papers from Budapest, from your father. She tried to get in, and of course, no such luck. They kept her back. But then Meier, this was the name of this non-Jewish go-between-- took me to his room, and he explained what had had happened. Margit had come-- Margit-- the papers arrived three days after I had left Amsterdam. With my Hungarian father, papers from the Hungarian president, from the Nazis, from the German, all totally legal. Illegal but legal. And Margit tried to get permission to travel, to bring them to me. Of course, she didn't get permission. So she went also illegal. Got arrested at the border, slipped herself through with a couple of German soldiers, who brought her to me, to Belgium. And she thought they would really release me. She would get to the kommandant, and give him the papers, and say, you've got to let her go. She doesn't belong here. She didn't manage to do so, because the kommandant refused to see her. So the only thing she got to do is she let me know that she was going to Brussels to see the big, big, big Nazi from the Belgian thing, by the name of Erdman, E-R-D-M-A-N, to get him the papers to see that I'd be released. And she left. And I got a card from her, from Brussels, that the man she wanted to see was on his way to Berlin, but she handed it to someone else, whatever. And I was reading the card outside, on the staircase, with two of my friends-- three, actually. We were sharing the last cigarette between us, and suddenly Meier comes running up and says, go, go, go back to your hall, back to your hall. Boden is coming. He's in a foul mood. And before we knew it, Boden came up the stairs, screaming. I had a night gown on, and from the other guy, the raincoat. We were just talking about Margit being here, and that's all. And it was pouring, pouring rain. And he chased us down in the courtyard. Three guys, and me in night clothes. And then the entire hall. And he put us through two hours-- the hall just had to march around, barefoot, in the rain. The others in the hall, with spotlights on us. I was sure the whole world can see me. The raincoat fell off immediately. I wore a very decote-- it's the only thing I had with me. Two hours of the worst exercise you can imagine. And I was in good, good shape. I'd been a dancer. I mean, really physically in excellent shape. But it was almost impossible, because it went faster, and faster, and down and up and down, and up and knee bends, and knee bends, and you know. And he stood right next to me. And after a while, Boden, with his boots, I could see-- I knew exactly, he wouldn't stop. He wouldn't stop until I was face-down on the cement, laying on the stones. He would not stop-- he wanted me down. And I knew I couldn't stop. And then came a time when I couldn't breathe anymore. And the Belgian Nazi pulled me-- I had very long hair-- pulled me by the hair, like a puppet, up and then tossed me down. Up and down, up-- and I don't know whether he wanted to help me or kill me. I have no idea. But I was dangling in the air. And he put me down. And then the kommandant threw water down on us and made him stop. And I passed out kind of halfway. And he kept saying, whore, whore, with three men. You know, night party, and things like that, over the loudspeaker. And the next morning, over the loudspeaker, to make sure that everybody could hear that he has found, and these were three student elders there. In other words, who were in charge of that thing, and one woman, had a little orgy there. And to discourage others from doing so, he ordered [NON-ENGLISH]. The Belgians could get packages-- we, from Holland, couldn't get anything. But they could get clean clothing and books or whatever. So-- and the cigarette ration-- which were, I think, Swiss cigarettes-- a week. Because-- and if they didn't like it, they could come to me and complain.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very nice. I couldn't move at all. I mean, I had muscles I didn't even know I had. I literally could not move.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet I went down the next morning-- don't me ask me how. I remember what we did as children, my twin and I, when it was too boring for us to slide down the banister, we slid down on the-- on the steps, you know. And that's how I got down. I remember this from my childhood. However, I couldn't figure out why I had liked that when I was a child. It wasn't really all that funny. Down the steps, into the gym class-- you know, where they had gymnastic. I didn't want him to think that I-- I couldn't make it. And he saw me, and he released me. He told me I could go up and rest. And from that moment on, he never bothered me again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they had something else that which-- for the first time I realized-- they had a show there every Sunday. Now, I didn't take plays a long time because there was rain, it was always rained out. And of course, it had to be in the courtyard. There was--you know, in every camp, you have plenty of carpenters, you have plenty of-- actors and musicians. They were always there. They used to have every Sunday a show. They built a stage outside, and there was entertainment. And everybody was looking forward to it, of course. What I did not know, that this entertainment was also frequented by the Nazis, themselves-- by the commander, by his-- by Boden, by other people. I was asked to perform, and I said no. And then I was ordered to perform. I mean, absolutely ordered, or else, again. And what happened is that just-- I said, then yes. I figured I'll give the worst performance of my life, that way they will never ask me again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, who can fault me? They said I had to perform. They didn't say it had to be good.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day of the performance, let me tell you, I had no idea. I looked down, and the whole two rows were full of those medal-studded Nazis. Like they were poured into their uniforms. I mean, ghouls. And they were sitting there, I mean, with the expression, entertain me. You know, do something for me. And I saw these guys. And I heard the music, and I froze. Nothing came out. I didn't have to pretend. Believe me, I didn't have to pretend. I did nothing. The music started playing and started playing. And then one of them called out to me-- it was like a freak show. Dance! Dance for us! And I started dancing-- I had choreographed something. No idea what I did. I mean, totally irrational, totally stupid. The only thing I remember, I ended up in a split, which was the only thing I had choreographed, anyway. And then stayed down, until Meier came and took me off. Well, it is true, they never asked me again. However, they really thought I did it on purpose, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and took away my E4 number, decision, and gave me a transport number. Now, I have no proof that this was in conjunction, this one followed because of the other. But there was no other reason for it. None. And then it happened that the camp was full, and full, and fuller. And rumor started to spread. Rumor started to spread that there were people among who had always escaped. There was a certain spot in Belgium where the train was going very slowly, uphill. And this was a spot where you could jump, disappear into the woods. Underground was notified. They were there waiting for you. It seems that these transport from Mechelen, wherever their destination was, there was always much less arrived than had left Mechelen. And after many, many, many, many months of this, it seems that Berlin started very worried about it, or angry about it, or whatever. And they ordered, first of all, that the camp-- which had never happened before-- has to be emptied out completely. Everybody leaves. And then that there is a lot, a lot of soldiers with guns on every wagon. And that Erdman himself, the big boss, is travelling from A to Z, with them. The rumor started, also, that when there was a transport before, they always gave you a few days and said in three, four days, be prepared, we go. This time, there was no such thing. It was like you were sitting on a-- on a powder keg. You could feel the tension. And there were men in there which had escaped before. Some of them made it. Some of them were brought back again, put in this cold cellar for a few days, for exactly as long as they were free-- they had to be in the cold cellar. And then they went back. And they told others where to go, how to go. In other words, the ones who wanted to escape would practice on the beams how to jump, how to lie under a train, how to be on the side. All of it. And everybody knew it, including the kommandant. Nobody stopped anybody. But what they said was, this time, whoever can escape, if they make it, we'll get-- the commander will give them the iron cross for making it. It was an incredible atmosphere there. You could feel it-- something was happening. It was much too full. It was about twice, three times the size of what it usually was. And then the day came, then they said everybody has to leave. The only ones they held back was my friend Martin. They decided-- the kommandant decided he ought to train the new German Shepherd dogs which came to attack people. He never had a dog in his life. He knew nothing about dogs. He was scared of them. And the other one was a cook. A girl, cook. Everybody else had to go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The kommandant held a speech. And if somebody would ask me to tell them under oath, did the kommandant know where we were going, or did he not, or did he believe what he was saying, I could not tell you. He was extremely sincere. I have no-- after all these years, I couldn't tell you. He said we were going to a brand new camp, a labor camp. We had to labor, we had to work. But we had to finish it, because it wasn't finished. We had medical, good food. Much better than this crowded Mechelen, and much more facilities there. And we could choose the jobs we have. We could even choose a mayor. So everything, it was up to us. Much better than here. And we would be treated very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now as I said, I have no idea. Did he know where we were going? I don't know. I just know that there was a horrendous amount of soldiers with guns-- a horrendous amount of soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody saw the cars, the cattle cars, at all. It was like-- like on a rotating movie set. You suddenly rotate, and there, that it was nothing before, through the gate, suddenly there is the very, very quickly laid tracks, and cattle cars-- for us, the people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't know how many soldiers were going with us. We didn't know whether they were there just there to-- as the one said, step right up lady, step right up, lady. Like on a carnival. You know, the next ride. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very, very hot. It was the middle of summer, middle of August. Incredibly hot. No water. And too crowded, everything, of course. Nobody knew where or what. They-- they put the cartons with us. There was a separate wagon for Erdman, for the big shots-- also separate from all the others. My group, of course, we had-- I had practiced escape, practiced from beam to beam under the thing, and all of it. This cattle car ride, which now everybody who knows about Auschwitz knows about, or knows about cattle car drive, has been talked about and written about a million times-- can't even be-- come close to be described. First of all, now you're fenced in. Now there is that little bit of a window. Like a monkey in a zoo, looking out. And it is in one minute, so hot, that even the most prudish-- they have to undress. You can't help it. We have to undress. Of course, no facilities at all. There's a bucket. We had a women sitting on the bucket from the first moment on, all dressed in black. I had never seen her before, I have no idea who she was. She was like a character out of a Strindberg play. You know, somebody who'd forgotten her lines, but was still wearing costume. And she sat there. She sat there all through it. And any time somebody needed to use the bucket, she would get up, turn her back, and the moment that was finished, she would sit again. She would not-- only her hands were moving all the time. Somebody said that she had a son, a young son, in another wagon, who wanted to jump. And she didn't want to see him get killed, so she had chosen-- chosen this wagon. My friends and I had decided-- practiced-- that we could lay under the wagon, under the train. There was plenty of room. Let the train go over us. And then, at this particular spot-- which is a spot which we had practiced and seen in our mind a thousand times-- you know, like you look at the same picture. You know exactly where it is, and what you have to do. You have to run like the wind down the slopes and disappear in the woods, where then, the underground is, will tell you where to go, what to do, and so forth. It's only on this spot, where the train goes very, very slow-- like when steep uphill. That's where you go under the train. Now, where I was sitting, like I'm sitting here, on this one wall, there was this much of a board, which my friends saw we had, the last week was smuggled in, of course, all sorts of, you know, saw and hammer and things like this. And it was interesting. Nobody cared. Boden didn't care, the kommandant didn't care, because he knew there would be jumpers.
